title: Mobile receipt scanning on the go

turns.speaker: Marcus
turns.text: Ray, you're on the road a lot. How do you use Maple day to day?

turns.speaker: Ray
turns.text: Almost entirely on my phone. I'm at job sites, gas stations, hardware stores. I snap a photo of the receipt right at the register and move on. Or that's the idea, anyway.

turns.speaker: Marcus
turns.text: You said 'that's the idea.' What actually happens?

turns.speaker: Ray
turns.text: The receipt scanning gets the total wrong a lot. Like it reads $42.00 as $4.20, or it grabs the wrong date. Crumpled gas receipts are basically a coin flip. Then I'm fixing numbers later at night, which defeats the whole point of scanning on the spot.

turns.speaker: Marcus
turns.text: How often would you say the scan is wrong?

turns.speaker: Ray
turns.text: Maybe one in three needs a correction. And the app doesn't flag which ones it's unsure about, so I have to double-check all of them. If it just told me 'low confidence on this amount,' I'd only check those.

turns.speaker: Marcus
turns.text: Anything else about the mobile experience?

turns.speaker: Ray
turns.text: The camera capture is slow to open, and if I have spotty signal at a site the photo sometimes fails to upload and I don't notice until later. I've lost a couple of receipts that way. Offline capture that syncs when I get signal would be huge.

turns.speaker: Marcus
turns.text: Despite all that, you keep using it. Why?

turns.speaker: Ray
turns.text: Because taking a photo still beats keeping a shoebox of paper. It's better than what I did before. But the OCR accuracy is the thing that would make me actually trust it instead of babysitting every scan.
